observations.recommendation:
6.16	We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
6.16.1	Require the City Engineer’s Office (CEO) to submit a Statement of Work Accomplished and the Inspection Report on the above-mentioned projects under Construction-in-Progress accounts;
6.16.2	Require the City Accounting Department to effect the necessary adjustments on the accounts affected once the CEO submit the reports. Adjust those transactions that were improperly capitalized; and
6.16.3	Likewise, ensure that all completed projects are properly recognized in the appropriate PPE accounts.

8.9	We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
8.9.1	Direct the Head of the City Health Office to see to it that fidelity bonds of accountable officers are renewed annually; and
8.9.2	Require the City Treasurer’s Office to keep a record of the expiry period of all fidelity bonds of accountable officers for proper monitoring.

9.13	We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following:
9.13.1	Require the Accounting Department to revert to the unappropriated    surplus, the over two years old Other Payables- 10% Retention Fees of ₱394.538.90 and Other Payables – Others totaling ₱2,549.00, totaling ₱397,087.90; and
9.13.2	Strictly monitor the retention fees and other payables to comply with the provisions of the above-mentioned laws, rules and regulations.

12.9	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should require the concerned officials:
12.9.1	To submit to COA the application and pertinent required documents for the request for relief from money and/or property accountability in compliance with COA memorandum no. 92-751 dated February 24, 1992;
12.9.2	Require to submit satisfactory explanation or reason for delay of filing of request; and
12.9.3	As a way forward, management is encouraged to ensure that appropriate request for relief from accountability be immediately filed before COA for any loss of government funds and property.

13.9	We recommended the following:
Accountable Officer
13.9.1	To restitute immediately the full amount of misappropriated fund, otherwise charges for malversation of public funds may be filed against them with the corresponding administrative sanction;
Management
13.9.2	To discontinue the practice of granting additional cash advances to accountable officer without liquidating first their previous cash advance;
13.9.3	To file a claim for payment from the Fidelity Fund with the Bureau of Treasury pursuant to Treasury Circular no. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009; and
13.9.4	To instruct the incumbent accountable officers to comply with Section 101 of P.D 1445 and the Internal Audit Services to review and strengthen its internal control and process flows in order to avoid incurrence of shortage/overage in the future.

14.7	We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to:
14.7.1	Require the City Accountant to comply with the prescribed period of    submission to enable the Office of the City Auditor to fully execute its review and evaluation and to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension and imposition of sanction provided under Section 122 of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and
14.7.2	If possible, hire additional staff to assist the office of the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended Management to:

1) Exhaust all means possible for the collections of dormant receivable account; and

2) Require the Head of the Agency to file the request for authority to write-off of dormant accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers together with the attached supporting documents as provided under Par. 7.4 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant requested assistance from COA in identifying the recorded disallowances in the books and in determining whether they are already final and executory.
observations.reason: The Office of the City Accountant is having difficulty in retrieving details of the disallowances which were booked as beginning balance of the Receivables-Disallowances/Charges account when the Electronic New Government Accounting System (e-NGAS) was installed 12 years ago.  The beginning balance on August 2006 includes only names of employees/suppliers with corresponding disallowed amount but with no particulars or details regarding the disallowance and physical records are no longer available despite efforts to locate them.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, as the one who is responsible for all government funds and property of the city government, should require the City Accountant and City Budget Officer to submit the financial reports and supporting schedules/documents in such form and within the period prescribed in Section 4.1 of COA Circular 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended the City Mayor to direct:
The City Treasurer to submit the Schedule of Unreleased Checks to the City Accountant specially at year-end.
The City Accountant to do the following remedial measures to attain fairness of presentation of the Cash and Cash Equivalent, in conformity with Paragraph 76 of IPSAS 1:
Discontinue the inclusion of unreleased checks among its outstanding checks in preparing its Bank Reconciliation Statements; and
Prepare the JEV to restore the cash and liabilities pertaining to the unreleased checks.

We recommended that Management should:
1. 	Prepare and submit monthly BRS within the prescribed period for review as required under Section 74 and section 112 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011;
2. 	Request the depository bank to provide the agency with the copy of the bank statements within five days after the end of the month. If not possible, request electronic copies (snapshots) of the transactions of the bank accounts to facilitate the immediate preparation of the monthly BRS report; and
3. 	Cause the timely preparation of monthly BRS so that errors/omissions and/or reconciling items would be immediately detected for correction and adjustment in the books of accounts, thus ensuring the accuracy and reliability of the Cash in Bank balances in the agency’s trial balance and financial statements.
4. 	Instruct the City Accountant to evaluate the efficiency and directly supervise the work output of its personnel and staff and ensure that their duties and responsibilities are carried out in a timely manner in order not to cause significant delays in its accounting and reporting functions.

We recommended the management to:
Require the Accountable Officer (AO) to:
Prepare Report on Disbursement (ROD) attached with complete supporting documents;
Require the prompt preparation and submission of the Liquidation Reports together with the required supporting documents; and
Return immediately any excess/unutilized cash and cash equivalents by the end of the month.
Require the CTO to:
Regularly conduct cash count to its accountable officer to ensure that cash is intact.
Review the ROD with its corresponding supporting documents.
Maintain a monitoring list of the cash advances granted to the accountable officers.
Require the Accounting Office to:
Demand accountable officers to liquidate all their cash advances with complete supporting documents at the end of the year and require them to refund immediately any unexpended balance;
Discontinue the practice of granting additional cash advances to accountable officer without liquidating first their previous cash advance;
Regularly update the subsidiary ledger to ensure that accountable officer’s cash advance does not exceed the maximum cash accountability; and
Closely monitor the granting and liquidation of cash advance to ensure compliance with the provision of COA Circular 97-002.

Recommendation:
We hereby recommend the Local Chief Executive:
To submit the list of the specific role of each participant that is in line with their functions/mandates in their respective official station and the objective of the week-long event;
To direct all thirty-six (36) participants to submit an accomplishment report in accordance with the specific roles assigned to them, and a narrative on the learnings they have gained, indicating how these would benefit their mandates/functions in their office station; and
To discontinue the practice of granting foreign travel authority to those persons excluded in Sec. 3 of EO 77 and submit a written justification why those Job Order/Contract of Service employees were allowed/granted foreign travel authority.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive:
To discontinue the practice of transferring cash advances from one Accountable Officer (AO) to another, and instead require his LGU officials and employees to be granted such cash advances under their names for their future official travels pursuant to Items 3.2 and 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
To instruct the LGU officials and employees to strictly comply with COA Circular 96-004 dated April 19, 1996, wherein immediate refund of cash advances should be made in case an official travel is cancelled or terminated in advance and to direct the prevention on the use of cash advances for purposes other than it was supposedly intended.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive:
To require the LGU employees from Table F above to submit a written justification allowing them to extend their foreign travel and to submit other clearances that would justify the deviation from the approved travel time;
To direct the City Accountant to submit the required Certification that previous cash advances have been fully liquidated and accounted for in the books as shown in Table G and to submit the required supporting documents noted in items 1.3, 2.2 and 2.3 above;
To submit a written justification why the submitted Official Foreign Travel Authority was signed and approved only by the DepEd Region X - Regional Director; and
To be prudent in granting Foreign Travel Authorities to its officers and employees especially in the compliance of Executive Order No. 77, Memorandum Circular No. 35 and other related laws, rules and regulations concerning the expenditures for foreign travel.

We recommended that the Management to strictly adhere to the mandated use of government plates and markings, and to direct the General Service Office to mark all City-owned vehicles with the marking “For Official Use Only” (3 inches), under which shall be written with the name of the City Government of Gingoog, in conformity with COA Circular No. 77-61.
We also suggested the Management to prepare an action plan to facilitate the application with Land Transportation Office (LTO) for conversion of the registration of above-mentioned vehicles that do not carry the prescribed plate from private to red plate.

We recommend the City Government to direct:
The concerned Department Head to immediately inform the concerned suppliers/contractors to submit a formal letter request for release of retention money in accordance with the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A No. 9184; and
If the concerned suppliers/contractors fail to submit a formal letter request for release of retention money within the deadline set by the management, require the City Accountant to adjusting journal entries in the books to correct the erroneous recording of accounts.

10.10	We recommended the Management to direct the following:
10.10.1	City Accounting Department and General Services Department
Reconcile the discrepancy between accounting records and RPCPPE to ascertain the accuracy and completeness of the amount and PPE recorded in the books; and
Conduct investigation on the causes of the discrepancy and explanations shall be required from the person responsible.

10.10.2	City Accounting Department
Reconcile the PPE subsidiary ledgers and lapsing schedule.
Maintain perpetual inventory records of PPELC for each category of property, plant, and equipment.
Record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation with GSD to     establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts.
Strictly comply with Section 114, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for   LGUs.

10.10.3	General Services Department
Record, monitor and account all properties, real or personal, owned by the City whether acquired through procurement or donation as provided in Section 490(b)(3)(l) of RA No. 7160 – The Local Government Code of the Philippines.
Ensure that only PPE items shall be included in the RPCPPE.
Henceforth, comply strictly with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386; and Section 491(i)(b) of the GAAM, Volume I.
Conduct the required inventory taking of PPE every year as required and update records of the RPCPPE as of CY 2023.

11.9.1	We recommended the management to instruct the DepEd Supply Officer to ensure that all issuances of Property, Plant & Equipment items are covered with Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs) as prescribed under Section 58 to Government Accounting Manual for LGUs Volume II.
11.9.2	Likewise, issue PARs on all equipment listed herein to establish accountability of end-users. Ensure end-users duly acknowledge receipt of property by signing the received portion with date received thereon.

12.10	We recommended the management to direct the Local School Board and the DepEd-Gingoog to ensure that all procurement for a given year should be in accordance with the time schedule for each procurement activity and contract implementation as programmed in the approved APP and Work and Financial Plan to prevent undue delay in their implementation at the expense of the Government and its education stakeholders.

13.12.1	We recommended the Gingoog City Local School Board and the DepEd Gingoog to:
Jointly exert more effort to fully implement related programs, projects and activities;
Maximize the utilization of the Special Education Fund in order to attain the objectives for which the funds were appropriated; and
Strictly adhere to DEPED-DBM-DILG Join Circular No. 1 s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 with emphasis on the required timeline in the preparation, review, and approval of the proposed SEF Budget in order to achieve the needs for the efficient operation and maintenance of public schools.

14.10.1	We recommended that the LCE direct the CDRRMO and City Planning & Development Coordinator to prepare and submit supplemental LDRRMFIP/LDRRMP for all PPAs to be charged to LDRRMF which were not yet included in the annual budget such as the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF under the special trust funds. Thereafter, the LDRRMFIP/LDRRMP shall be submitted to the CDC to be incorporated in the AIP for approval of the SP.

14.10.2	We also recommended that the LCE direct the CDRRMO and City Accountant to seek prior authorization of the LDRRMFIP before implementing any programs, projects and activities against the Unexpended LDRRMF – Special Trust Fund. when there is an approved AIP.

We have hereby recommended that Management instruct all resident and project engineers to closely monitor the implementation of the NTF-ELCAC funded projects to avoid substantial delay and negative slippages. Immediately send demand letter(s) to erring contractors to fast-track project completion, and compute and impose liquidated damages of the aforementioned projects to the erring contractors.

We recommended the City Accountant to: 

a) Verify the validity of outstanding payables aged two years and more, and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors. Valid claims shall be paid. Otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund; and 

b) Exert effort to retrieve the documents of unidentified payables or those without details in the subsidiary ledgers to properly classify the amounts recorded.

We recommended that LGU- City of Gingoog management through the City Accountant to notify and to require all persons held responsible/liable to settle immediately their disallowances/charges as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006.  Secondly, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive shall exert extra effort in collecting the receivables and initiate necessary administrative action, if warrants. Lastly, the City Accountant shall provide Allowance for Impairment – Receivables- Disallowances/Charges for non-collection of the disallowances and charges.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Accounting Department:
Prepare a monthly reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are recorded accordingly;
Perform timely review of transactions to deter error and omissions; and,
Exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled balances. If after exhausting all the possible means and the balances could not be accounted, request for write-off of the said accounts by complying the documentary requirements and procedures under COA Circular 2016-005.

We have recommended that management require the City Accounting Office to exert extra effort to make confirmation of recorded liabilities from the registered claimants, if any. Effect necessary adjustment to the liability accounts, when necessary. Otherwise, revert those undocumented accounts to the General Fund.

We have recommended that the City Accountant and the Acting City Treasurer to strictly observe the pertinent provisions of PD 1445 and the aforementioned COA Circulars in the preparation, recording of financial reports/documents, and the prompt submission of the said financial reports and transaction documents in compliance with the aforementioned rules and regulations.

We have recommended that management demand the immediate refund of the remaining unrecouped advances or deduct the amount from any claim due from the concerned contractor.
We further recommended that all officials and staffs involved in the processing of claims/billings ensure that advances to contractors are fully recouped upon completion of the projects and prior to payment of final billing.

We have recommended that the management direct the City Treasurer’s Office to open and maintain separate depository bank account to be used solely for the proceeds of collection from various NGOs/POs and intensify the collection/recovery of unpaid amortization(s). In case repayment is in default, issue demand letter to the beneficiary.
We further recommended that the CTO monitor quarterly its collection activity in order for the Accounting Section to perform timely and accurate account/payment reconciliation.
We further recommended that the City Agriculturist Office shall see to it that the beneficiary of the livelihood/financial assistance submits monthly fund utilization and physical status reports, copy furnish the COA for audit evaluation purposes.

We have recommended that management furnish the Auditor copy of the purchase orders and contract documents not later than five (5) working days from issuance of purchase orders or receipt of purchase order by the supplier together with duly accomplished supporting documents for technical review and evaluation in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001. Also, require the designated City General Services Officer (CGSO) to immediately furnish the Office of the Auditor a copy of Acceptance & Inspection Report within twenty-four (24) hours after acceptance on all deliveries and/or soon after the inspection and acceptance by the LGU personnel concerned so that a timely inspection can be made by the COA.

We have recommended that the Management require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to cause immediate submission of the lacking documents as indicated in “Annex D” for evaluation purposes.
We also recommended that the BAC to conscientiously check/review the completeness of the legal, technical and financial documents submitted to ascertain the regularity and propriety of the award of contract. Moving forward, ensure submission of complete documents for succeeding contracts as prescribed on the aforementioned COA Circulars to facilitate review of contracts and determine compliance with R.A. 9184.

We have recommended that management require the concerned Project/Resident Engineer to compute and impose liquidated damages of the aforementioned projects to the erring contractors, copy furnish the Accounting Department and COA.
We also recommended that management direct the Project/Resident Engineers to strictly monitor and supervise the City’s infrastructure projects being implemented and compute actual slippage and liquidated damages to erring contractors if the project is not yet completed after target date for future projects and contracts.

We have recommended that management ensure that procurement not related to capital expenditures or development projects should be charged to its proper fund. Further, the City Accountant and City Budget Officer properly review expenditures charged to 20% City Development Fund and refrain or discontinue the practice of utilizing the fund for procurement not in accordance with the primary purpose for which the fund was established.

We have recommended that the management through the DepEd will expedite the distribution/utilization of the said idle IT equipment as these are prone to deterioration and possible damage.
We further recommend to ensure that IT equipment related to education be delivered/utilized immediately after acceptance and inspection by the inventory committee and/or end-user to address and improve the quality of education, and to also enjoy the benefit of warranty period in case malfunction will occur.

We have recommended that Management:
Require the proponents to submit the lacking supporting documents in order to prove the validity and propriety of the disbursements on or before February 15, 2022; and,
Discontinue the existing practice of granting educational researches to DepED employees without observing the guidelines set forth in the Department Order No. 16, s. 2017 dated March 20, 2017.

We have recommended that the City Accountant secure a copy of the MOA/Trust Agreements from the source agencies and review the terms and conditions corresponding to the funds received. Assign an accounting personnel to verify the implementation of specific programs or projects or determine whether goods/services were delivered, liquidation of funds were already submitted and received by the source agencies, and liabilities were already settled for proper adjustments in the books of accounts. Further, return to the source agency or remit to the Bureau of Treasury any unused or unexpended balance if so stipulated in the MOA/Trust Agreement.

We have also recommended that the City Accounting Office conduct regular monitoring of the status on the individual subsidiary ledger accounts composing the Due to NGAs account.

We have recommended that the City Engineering Office and other lead offices of the City such as CPDO, City Agriculture Office, CEED and ENRU to prioritize and fast track the implementation of the above-programmed development projects under the 20% LDF.
observations.reason: To identify the projects not yet implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City management directs the City General Services Officer to dutifully furnish on a monthly basis the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the City Accountant to properly record the expense accounts on the period which inventories are issued for consumption or utilization. Secondly, we recommend constituting an inventory committee and instructing them to conduct an annual physical count on all inventory accounts and reconcile the same with the accounting records periodically to reflect a reliable inventory balances.
observations.reason:
Supporting documents of some inventory accounts especially to those non-moving items on the inventory schedule could not anymore be located in the office files which result to difficulties in making the related SSMI.

SSMI of inventory accounts with complete supporting documents were prepared and submitted to Accounting Office when payment has been made and already posted in the schedule of inventory accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City management directs the City Accountant through the Fund Accounting staff in charge to regularly monitor the progress especially the completion of the CIP and coordinate with the City Engineering Office or any other Office implementing the project for proper reclassification to appropriate PPE accounts upon its completion. If found to be completed, appropriately reclassify the account to the PPE and properly estimate the depreciation cost of the mentioned PPE based on the existing accounting regulation on depreciation.

Secondly, the LGU has to scrutinize and prioritize laudable projects/programs to be implemented to maximize the benefits availment by the constituents. observations.managementAction: Final demand letters were already sent to the concerned barangays and NGOs/POs and will continue to follow up the submission of the lacking supporting documents. Some of these accounts were already liquidated.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the City Government send a final notice requiring the concerned Barangays to submit the required liquidation of the funds transferred. Further, we recommend that the City Engineering Office and the City Project Monitoring and Evaluation Committee be required to conduct inspection and validation of the extent of project implementation and require a Certificate of Completion and Final Inspection Report if the project is found satisfactorily completed as the basis for liquidation.

We have also recommended to consider disqualification of the defaulting Barangays from receiving subsequent fund transfers from the City Government.

We have recommended that the City Government send a final demand letter requiring various NGOs/POs to submit the required liquidation of the funds received. Otherwise, institute legal action against the defaulting NGOs/POs.

We have also recommended to consider disqualification of the defaulting NGOs/POs from receiving subsequent livelihood assistance from any government institution.

We have recommended that the City Accountant secure a copy of the MOA/Trust Agreements from the source agencies and review the terms and conditions corresponding to the funds received. Assign an accounting personnel to verify the implementation of specific programs or projects or determine whether goods/services were delivered, liquidation of funds were already submitted and received by the source agencies, and liabilities were already settled for proper adjustments in the books of accounts. Further, return to the source agency or remit to the Bureau of Treasury any unused or unexpended balance if so stipulated in the MOA/Trust Agreement.

We have also recommended that the City Accounting Office conduct regular monitoring of the status on the individual subsidiary ledger accounts composing the Due to NGAs account.

We have recommended that the City Accountant determine the correct cost of undistributed/unconsumed goods and make necessary adjustment(s) or revert back the same to “Welfare Goods for Distribution” account in conformity to Section 190, Volume I of GAM for LGUs.

We further recommended that the Accounting Office and GSO to conduct periodic inventory reconciliation of accounting and end-user records after the correct cost of inventory is established.

We have recommended that Management ensure that the charging of fuel expenditures to 20% Local Development Fund are in accordance with the guidelines provided under DILG-DBM JMC No.2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 in order that the primary and intended purpose of the fund is achieved. Also, revert back to the General Fund the amount of all fuel expenditures purely administrative in nature which were inappropriately charged against the 20% Development Fund.

We have also recommended to instruct all the drivers and operators concerned to completely indicate the required data in the Driver’s Trip Ticket, particularly those information supporting the daily fuel consumption. Also, require the approving officer to verify the accuracy of the fuel consumption reported by the drivers and operators prior to submission in the accounting department.

We have recommended that Management strictly observe the pertinent provisions of various GPPB issuances relative to the procurement of COVID-19 related projects in the subsequent transactions. Direct the BAC Secretariat to post immediately on the GPPB online portal all contracts awarded through Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act, regardless of the funding source, for the public to fully access procurement information. Further, post and publicized in any conspicuous place within the premises of the PE the information/data relative to COVID-19 fund sources and its utilization in order for the constituents of the City be fully inform and have knowledge on the proper accounting of government funds especially in this time of pandemic.

We have recommended that Management submit to the Auditor copy of all infrastructure contracts and its supporting documents within five (5) working days from its execution in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We have recommended that Management require the Supply and/or Property Custodian of the General Services Office to indicate in the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) the date of the actual delivery of the goods procured and to strictly require the suppliers to indicate the delivery date in their respective delivery receipts and/or charge invoices.

We have also recommended to strictly implement the imposition of liquidated damages to suppliers who failed to satisfactorily deliver the goods within the specified delivery schedule, as provided for in the contract.

We have recommended that the City Government cause the imposition of liquidated damages to be deducted from the claims of the concerned contractors. Notify the concerned contractors for possible termination/rescission of contracts on infrastructure projects particularly those with liquidated damages that reached 10% and negative slippages of more than 15% arising from contractor’s fault or negligence, or those determined with prima facie evidence that the contractors have engaged in unlawful acts pursuant to Annex I of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184. Further, institute legal action, if warranted, to the erring contractors and/or LGU employees who are engaged in unlawful acts relative to the project implementation.

We have also recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee recommend for possible disqualification and/or blacklisting of the above contractors.

Furthermore, we recommended that the City Engineering Office strictly monitor and supervise regularly the project implementation to minimize the delays in the completion of various projects of the LGU.

We have recommended that the City Government thru the Local School Board refrain from allocating “initial only” or partial funding from the SEF for school building construction. Ensure that adequate funds be provided in order for the classroom to be completed and can be used for academic purposes.

We have recommended that the City Engineering Office and other lead offices of the City such as CPDO, City Agriculture Office, CEED and ENRU to prioritize and fast track the implementation of the above-programmed development projects under the 20% LDF.
Annual Audit Report CY 2019

We recommended that the City management directs the City Treasurer to properly account the SEF and TF cash deposited in HYSA 2121-1142-43 and withdraw and deposit the TF amount to another HYSA account to be accounted under the Trust Fund to be approved by the Sangguniang Panglungsod.  Likewise, it is recommended that the City Accountant shall draw a journal entry for the correcting transactions of the SEF and TF HYSA accounts to properly account and reflect an accurate bank balance of the two separate funds.

We recommended to the City management to direct the Acting City Treasurer to open an account with the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) exclusive for the Supplemental Feeding and Senior Citizen Pension Programs as required in the MOA and compliant to DSWD Administrative Order No. 4.
observations.managementAction: Per consultation with Ms. Rosalinda Bucton, Head CSWDO, the fund for supplemental feeding and social pension programs were no longer downloaded by DSWD to LGU Gingoog since Aug. 2018 and June 2020 respectively. This is also evident as per subsidiary ledger in the City Treasurer’s Office. The DSWD Regional Office had already handled the implementation of these projects.  In effect, the CSWD no longer need to request the SP/ Council for authorization of the City Mayor and City treasurer to open an account.

observations.managementAction:
The delayed implementation of these projects were due to the following reasons:

2014 BUB funds were downloaded to CLGU-Gingoog late in 2016 and so with the conduct of procurement process. During this time, our office conducted on-site consultation prior to project implementation and we found out that there were identified recipients died, transferred of residency and waived out from the project. To identify new beneficiaries will undergo a long process because a resolution from association and LPRAT are required before Sanguniang Panlungsod (SP) resolution will be executed;
In the procurement process, another predicament was encountered. The price qoutations of the suppliers are greater than the bid price. Thus, BUB funds were deficit to purchase the required quantities of materials/ equipment and labor/ rental. This is mainly contributed by the price escalation because the projects proposals were prepared and approved based on 2014 prevailing prices and the procurement conduct late in 2016. And so, the previous administration decided to return the unexpended balances to the Department of Agriculture (DA) Region 10;
Nevertheless, the current administration chose not to return the said fund, but to implement rather the project. Our office then, initiated the additional local funds as augmentation of total amount increased and requested the Local Chief Executive (LCE) and appropriation of the amount needed as fund augmentation Again, this process entails ample of time before Appropriation Ordinance (AO) will be issued.
Currently, all PRs and POs are on-process and we are waiting for its approval prior to the full implementation of the projects which includes Eight (8) various farmers associations and projects, to wit:
1. Expansion of Lakatan Production (Agay-agay)- Voucher on process
2. Farm Equipment (Brgy. Lunao)- Voucher on process
3. Corn Production (Brgy. Kalagonoy)- for quotation
4. Post-Harvest Facilities (Brgy. Kalagonoy)- For Quotation
5. Corn Production (Brgy. Malinao)- Voucher on process
6. Rice Production (Brgy. Malinao)- Voucher on process
7. Organic Rice and Corn (City Wide); and- Voucher on process
8. Sustainable Agri-based Livelihood Program- For Qoutation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to evaluate the individual account lodged in the Due from LGUs account and determine its possible settlement or collectability and to coordinate with the City Engineering Office and the City Planning and Development Office and trace the project that remained outstanding and determine the extent of its implementation; and lastly, prepare the necessary documentation to support the request for write-off of the long overdue accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant a) to evaluate and  determine the possible settlement of the accounts lodged in the Due from NGOs/ POs; b) coordinate with the lead office/s recommending the approval; c) revisit the Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) specifically the terms and obligations of the recipients; d) investigate the possibility of writing off the accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and e) prepare the appropriate adjustments through the journal entry vouchers (JEVs), applicable.

We recommended that LGU- City of Gingoog management through the City Accountant to notify and to require all persons held responsible/liable to settle immediately their disallowances/charges as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006.  Secondly, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive shall exert extra effort in collecting the receivables and initiate necessary administrative action, if warrants. Lastly, the City Accountant shall provide Allowance for Impairment – Receivables- Disallowances/Charges for non collection of the disallowances and charges.

We recommended that Management direct the City General Services Officer to dutifully prepare and furnish on a monthly basis the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the City Accountant for recording of appropriate expense accounts on the period which inventories are issued for consumption to end-users. Secondly, require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual physical count on all inventory accounts and reconcile the same with the accounting records. Lastly, require the City Accountant to write down the non-moving inventory items, if proven, by effecting the following entry:

We recommended requiring the City General Services Officer and the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory count of PPE as of December 31, 2018 to establish the physical existence and prepare and submit the RPCPPE to ascertain the accuracy and validity of the yearend balance. Secondly, require the City Accountant and GSO to maintain complete PPE Ledger Cards (PPELC) and property cards, respectively, and reconcile the difference of P934.34 million and effect the appropriate adjustments to fairly present the balance of the affected PPE.

We recommended to direct the different Accountable Officers to immediately request from Relief of Accountability whenever loss of properties through fire, theft/robbery or hold-up, force majeure and death of large cattle and other livestock  happened in accordance with the requirements of COA Memorandum No. 72-751 dated February 24, 1992.  Secondly, direct the General Service Officer to properly fill-up the Inspection and Inventory Report specifically Boxes 3 and 4 which are for COA representatives inspecting and witnessing the actual disposal of the unserviceable government properties.  Lastly, instruct the proper department personnel to improve the stock farm facilities in terms of cleanliness and security of the animals sheltered therein to prevent losing their lives through death caused by accidents and attacks of astray dogs and snake bites.

We reiterate our prior year’s recommendation to direct the City Accountant to re–evaluate the validity and existence of the individual balances of the Accounts Payable which are long outstanding for over two (2) years and likewise draw the necessary adjusting entries to revert the same to the unappropriated surplus if found to have no valid claims.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the Treasury Circulars regarding bonding of its public officials and employees by properly evaluating the total property accountabilities of each accountable officer and appropriately apply fidelity bond coverage based on the accountabilities handled.

We recommend that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere to Section 12.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report duly approved by the Head of the Procuring Entity to the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) in printed and electronic format within fourteen (14) days at the end of the semester to avoid sanctions of noncompliance and likewise furnish a copy of the PMRs duly acknowledged by the GPPB to the Office of the Auditor for review and evaluation.

We recommended that Management direct the City Budget Officer to coordinate with the City Planning and Development Coordinator to validate the SEF budget for integration with the AIP and APP as prescribed by Article 410 of the IRR of Republic Act No. 7160; and b) instruct the BAC to strictly adhere to Sections 7.1 and 7.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 and integrate the items in the SEF budget to its Annual Procurement Plan.

We recommend that LGU- City of Gingoog management through the General Services Office and the BAC should strictly track the procurement timelines provided in the various provisions of Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act 9184.  Likewise, indicate the specific brand name of the items to be procured in the Purchase Order.

We recommended that management shall address the reasons of the non-implementation of the 20% Development Fund by providing sufficient manpower for the planning and construction division to cope up with voluminous workloads.  Secondly, the LGU has to scrutinize and prioritize laudable projects/programs to be implemented to maximize the benefits availment by the constituents.  Lastly, closely monitor the implementation and completion of the line-up projects of the city.

We reiterate our prior year’s recommendation that Management direct the City Social Welfare and Development Officer (CSWDO) to strictly adhere to DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 17, series of 2015 and immediately release the downloaded funds to the recipients of the Social Pension Program in the first month of every quarter. Likewise, promptly submit the liquidation reports to the DSWD Regional Field Office to facilitate fast releases of funds for the ensuing quarters.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to regularly and promptly draw an adjustment thru journal entry voucher to all identified reconciling items immediately right after the preparation of monthly bank reconciliation statement to reflect an accurate bank balance in the financial statement as of a given period; and stop using the “unsubmitted vouchers” as reconciling item.  Likewise, require the City Treasurer to send a written notice to the payee of the existence of the check at least one month before a check becomes stale; marked all stale checks ‘cancelled” on its face; forward the original disbursement vouchers and supporting documents to the City Accountant to record related transactions as Accounts Payable; and request from the Auditor a certified copy of the disbursement voucher, in case the check became stale in the hand of the payee and due for replacement, for presentation to the City Official who shall countersign the new check.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Solid Waste Management Ten (10) Year Plan be reviewed and updated in accordance with the National Solid Waste Management Council (NSWMC) annotated outline as required under Section 16 of RA 9003.

We also recommended the full closure of the controlled dumpsite and the establishment of the sanitary landfill.

Moreover, to ensure efficiency and effectiveness of solid waste management program the following are recommended:

Establishment of Multi-Purpose Environment Cooperatives or Associations.
Used of cellophanes by all business owners/operators should be discouraged.
Segregation of waste as to biodegradable and non-biodegradable should be strictly followed by the residents/commercial establishments.
Impose strict compliance on collection of biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste pursuant to Section 21 of RA 9003. (Non-segregated or unsorted waste should not be collected)
Receptacles should be properly labelled as to biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste by the residents/commercial establishments.
Garbage should not be thrown in public places.
